frames.text: [!] CWE-732: VulnService path has weak ACL
[*] sc stop VulnService && sc start VulnService

question: CWE-732 (service binary replacement) works at standard user because:
choices: Windows services always run as SYSTEM regardless of the binary's ACL
The service manager can be tricked into running unsigned binaries
The service binary path has misconfigured ACLs — an unprivileged user has write access to the exe
UAC can be bypassed using the unquoted service path technique on any path
explanation: CWE-732: incorrect permission assignment. If BUILTIN\Users or a low-priv account has write/full-control on the service binary, you replace it with your own. On next service start (or reboot), SCM runs your binary as SYSTEM.

question: CWE-427 (phantom DLL hijack via ClickToRunSvc) exploits which Windows loader behaviour?
choices: The service's manifest lists DLLs that don't exist — Windows searches PATH before System32
DLL redirection via .local files overrides SysWOW64 loads
KnownDLLs can be overwritten by standard users if the registry key ACL is weak
AppInit_DLLs loads arbitrary code at process creation without elevation
explanation: If a service imports a DLL that isn't present, the loader walks the DLL search order. If any user-writable directory appears before System32 in that order, you plant the DLL there. ClickToRunSvc had a search-order gap a standard user could exploit.

prompt: winrecon checks 20 sections. Complete the PowerShell one-liner that finds writable service binaries:
hint: The three dangerous permission levels (pipe-separated regex)
explanation: FullControl, Write, or Modify on a service binary means a low-priv user can replace it. Any match here is a CWE-732 candidate.
wrong_hint: You want to match any of: FullControl, Write, or Modify.
